digest:
fctry is a Claude Code plugin that orchestrates eight specialized agents to convert conversational user visions into experience-first specifications in NLSpec v2 format and drive autonomous builds from them. The system enforces a strict experience language contract where the spec describes what the user sees, does, and feels, while all user-facing surfaces speak plain English through a single translation module. The eight commands—init, evolve, ref, review, execute, next, view, and stop—manage the full lifecycle from spec creation to autonomous execution. The spec is stored in `.fctry/spec.md` with a version registry in `.fctry/config.json` that auto-manages semver and propagates version changes to declared targets. The live spec viewer is a multi-project-aware local web server that renders markdown directly in the browser, supports real-time WebSocket updates, and transforms into mission control during builds to show progress, dependency graphs, and activity feeds. It features a kanban board for prioritization, dark mode, fuzzy search, and auto-generated Mermaid diagrams for entity relationships, user flows, and section dependencies.

The build process is plan-gated and autonomous after a single approval. The Executor breaks work into discrete chunks, manages context fidelity between dependent chunks, and handles retries and rearchitecting silently. The Observer agent verifies post-chunk output using browser automation tools like Rodney, Surf, and Peekaboo, or file inspection when those are unavailable. Build traces, lessons, and memory are persisted to support resumption and cross-session learning. The system enforces a quality ratchet that only accepts changes that improve scenario satisfaction or structural metrics, and it uses a four-tier severity vocabulary for findings. Tool validation checks for required tools like ripgrep, ast-grep, and MCP servers at startup.

The system is constrained by hard rules including experience language only, scenario holdout separation, and no human code review. Future directions include background-worker execution for parallel chunk processing, the viewer evolving into a control plane with a terminal emulator and MCP elicitation, a self-improvement loop via `/fctry:reflect`, and an experimental fully-autonomous loop. It also plans to add an arbiter-tier disagreement resolver for build-time verification, cross-project synthesis, and incremental build trace parsing.
